unit.name: Cartoning Machine
unit.cfj: Erect cartons and insert labeled sterile units into secondary packaging configurations

meta.method: Ulwick ODI (Importance × Satisfaction → Opportunity), needs scoring v2

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that erection, insertion, or closure compromises sterile-barrier integrity
rows.imp_rat: If erection, insertion, or closure compromises the sterile barrier, the core job fails at its purpose with catastrophic sterility and patient-safety consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Existing controls detect many but not all barrier compromises, leaving a residual failure mode with high consequence. Validating that erection, insertion, and closure preserve barrier integrity requires lengthy trials and testing rounds. Judging barrier-compromise risk needs specialized sterile-barrier and process knowledge that is not widely available. Barrier failures trigger expensive scrap, recalls, and investigations far exceeding the cost of prevention.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of sterile barriers damaged during carton erection, insertion, or closure
rows.imp_rat: Sterile barriers damaged during erection/insertion/closure are a direct CFJ failure; severe because barrier loss compromises sterility and patient safety.
rows.sat_rat: Inspection and validation reduce but do not eliminate barrier damage, so a measurable defect rate persists. Measuring barrier-damage rates across erection, insertion, and closure requires repeated sampling and inspection cycles. Interpreting damage causes at each machine step calls for experienced process and materials specialists. Damaged sterile barriers become rejected product, a direct and recurring material loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a sterile barrier or primary closure being breached during cartoning
rows.imp_rat: Breaching a sterile barrier or primary closure during cartoning is a direct CFJ failure with catastrophic sterility-loss consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Gentle-handling tooling reduces breaches but the status quo cannot guarantee barrier integrity through every transfer and closure. A breached sterile barrier is rarely detected in time to prevent it reaching packaging. Avoiding breaches during handling depends on careful setup and gentle transfer tuning by skilled staff. A breached sterile unit is a total loss and can trigger investigations, an expensive event.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of compromising sterile-unit container integrity during insertion or carton transport
rows.imp_rat: Compromising container integrity during insertion or transport defeats the protective purpose of cartoning with severe sterility and safety impact.
rows.sat_rat: Force-controlled inserters and guides help but insertion damage remains an intermittent, serious concern. Gentle handling settings do not materially add time once tuned. Tuning insertion to protect container integrity requires real mechanical skill. Compromised sterile units are high-value scrap and safety risks.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of sterile units damaged during insertion or enclosure
rows.imp_rat: Units damaged during insertion/enclosure is a central insertion-quality failure with severe consequences to product usability and sterility.
rows.sat_rat: Machine tuning and tooling reduce damage but variable product and handling conditions leave a persistent damage rate. Quantifying unit-damage during insertion means running production-representative trials that consume schedule. Diagnosing insertion damage requires machine-handling and product-fragility expertise concentrated in a few staff. Each damaged sterile unit is scrapped product, a direct financial loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a sterile-unit container closure being breached during cartoning
rows.imp_rat: Breaching a container closure during cartoning defeats sterility protection, the core purpose, with catastrophic patient-safety consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Machine handling reduces but does not eliminate the risk of closure breach during insertion. Closure integrity checks add minimal time during running. Preventing closure breaches depends on correct tooling and gentle handling design. A breached closure destroys sterility and the unit, a costly loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of compromising sterile-unit integrity while correcting an interruption
rows.imp_rat: Compromising unit integrity while correcting an interruption is a recovery-handling failure central to protecting product with severe sterility consequences.
rows.sat_rat: There is little standardized tooling ensuring integrity during interventions, so outcomes vary widely with the individual. Correcting an interruption while protecting sterile units takes time and often can't be rushed safely. Protecting sterile-unit integrity mid-intervention demands high operator judgement and steady technique that is scarce. Mishandled corrections scrap exposed units, a frequent recovery cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that carton identity does not match the enclosed labeled sterile unit or batch
rows.imp_rat: Carton identity not matching the enclosed unit or batch is a fundamental identity failure of the job with catastrophic traceability and safety impact.
rows.sat_rat: Reconciliation checks and batch controls are mature but the consequence severity keeps residual risk salient. Reconciling carton identity to enclosed unit adds verification time but is standard. Correct matching depends on disciplined reconciliation rather than deep expertise. A mismatch batch is costly to quarantine and reject.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of accepted sterile units with primary packages damaged during carton insertion
rows.imp_rat: Primary packages damaged during insertion is a direct failure of safe insertion, the heart of the CFJ, with sterility-breach consequences that are severe.
rows.sat_rat: Validated insertion tooling and gentle handling make insertion damage uncommon, though jams and misfeeds still cause sporadic damage. Insertion-related damage is usually caught at end-of-line inspection rather than instantly, adding some lag. Machine setup by skilled technicians reduces insertion damage, but tuning still relies on operator experience. Damaged units during insertion represent scrapped sterile product, a real but bounded cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of segregated rejected cartons returning to conforming output before reconciliation
rows.imp_rat: Segregated rejected cartons returning to conforming output before reconciliation reintroduces defective product, a severe quality-system failure.
rows.sat_rat: Quarantine and reconciliation procedures exist but rely on manual control that occasionally lets rejects slip back. Reconciliation before return of rejects adds procedural time. Requires disciplined tracking rather than special expertise. Rejects returning to good output risk costly quality failures.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of foreign material transferred onto sterile units during cartoning
rows.imp_rat: Foreign material transferred onto sterile units during cartoning is a direct contamination failure of clean handling with severe patient-safety impact.
rows.sat_rat: Existing enclosure, filtration and cleaning practices reduce but do not consistently eliminate foreign-material transfer onto sterile units. Foreign material transfer is detected late if at all, so operators cannot react in time during a run. Preventing transfer depends on cleanroom discipline and gowning judgement that vary by operator vigilance. Contaminated sterile units become scrapped batches, an expensive and hard-to-recover loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of sterile-unit primary containers being damaged during cartoning
rows.imp_rat: Damaging primary containers during cartoning defeats the protective purpose of the job and breaches sterility, a severe, non-recoverable failure.
rows.sat_rat: Well-designed cartoners handle containers gently and reliably, though wear, misalignment and jams intermittently damage containers. Damage prevention is designed into machine motion rather than requiring extra time. Correct machine change-parts and setup depend on skilled technicians to avoid pinch and crush points. Container damage during cartoning scraps product and consumes rework effort.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartoned sterile units with compromised primary packaging integrity
rows.imp_rat: Compromised primary packaging integrity directly defeats the sterile-unit protection the cartoning job exists to preserve; a breached sterile barrier is a patient-safety and recall-grade failure.
rows.sat_rat: Mature machine guarding, gentle-handling insertion stations and vision systems catch most integrity defects, though subtle compromises still escape occasionally. In-line vision and camera checks flag many integrity issues in real time, but full detection still requires downstream inspection that adds delay. Detecting compromised primary packaging depends partly on trained inspectors whose vigilance varies across shifts. Rejecting compromised units wastes sterile product and materials, a recurring but tolerated loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an incorrect carton, label, and sterile-unit combination remains undetected
rows.imp_rat: An incorrect carton/label/unit combination remaining undetected is a mix-up escape reaching patients, a severe and central detection failure.
rows.sat_rat: Camera/scanner checks catch most label-unit-carton mismatches, but coverage gaps let some pass undetected. Undetected mismatches surface late, requiring downstream rework. Detection increasingly relies on vision systems rather than operator skill. Undetected mismatches risk expensive recalls if they escape.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of sterile-unit defects remaining undetected before carton release
rows.imp_rat: Sterile-unit defects remaining undetected before release is a detection-gate escape delivering defective product to patients, a severe failure.
rows.sat_rat: Upstream inspection catches most defects but late-stage detection at cartoning is limited. Inspection for defects happens inline or at prior steps without extra time here. Detecting subtle sterile-unit defects before carton release demands trained inspection or reliable vision systems. Undetected defects reaching market are extremely costly, driving inspection investment.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that packaging-integrity defects escape detection before batch disposition
rows.imp_rat: Packaging-integrity defects escaping detection before disposition delivers breached product to market, a severe patient-safety and recall failure.
rows.sat_rat: Inline and sampling inspection catch many defects, but subtle integrity escapes before disposition remain a real gap. Integrity defect detection can lag until offline inspection or disposition. Catching escapes depends on inspector attention and sampling judgement. Escaped integrity defects create costly field failures and recalls.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that labeled sterile-unit identities and physical units become misaligned after a stop, restart, or jam recovery
rows.imp_rat: Misalignment of unit identities and physical units after a jam/restart is a core recovery-integrity failure with severe traceability/labeling consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Stop/restart/jam recovery is a well-known chronic failure point where identity-to-physical alignment routinely breaks despite procedures. Reestablishing identity alignment after a jam or stop consumes real recovery time on the line. Correct reconciliation after interruption depends on operator care and knowledge of recovery procedures that vary in quality. Misalignment after recovery causes scrapped or quarantined units, a frequent and tangible cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of units from different products, strengths, or lots being commingled during cartoning
rows.imp_rat: Commingling units of different products/strengths/lots during cartoning is a core mix-up hazard with catastrophic patient-safety and recall consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Formal line clearance and single-product staging make commingling uncommon, though residual human-dependent gaps persist. Line clearance procedures take time but prevent commingling before running. Preventing commingling relies heavily on disciplined operator line-clearance practice. Commingling triggers expensive batch rejection and investigation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of packaging materials from different products, batches, or lots becoming commingled
rows.imp_rat: Commingling packaging materials from different products/batches/lots is a mix-up hazard central to the job with catastrophic mislabeling and recall consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Line-clearance and single-lot staging practices are established but still depend on manual verification that occasionally fails. Preventing commingling adds line-clearance time between products. Segregating materials by product and lot requires disciplined operator adherence to procedures. Commingling events cause expensive batch rejections, driving strong prevention effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a market-specific cartoning configuration is released to an ineligible destination
rows.imp_rat: Releasing a market-specific configuration to an ineligible destination is a regulatory/market-authorization failure with catastrophic legal and recall impact.
rows.sat_rat: Configuration controls exist but market-eligibility errors are a persistent human-dependent weakness. Market-destination checks are often manual order-management steps outside the machine. Preventing wrong-market release relies on planning and QA judgement rather than automation. Wrong-market release risks costly recalls and regulatory penalties.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of rejected labeled sterile units entering accepted product flow
rows.imp_rat: Rejected labeled units entering accepted flow reintroduces defective product to market, a severe patient-safety and quality-system failure.
rows.sat_rat: Physical reject diversion is standard and effective, but buffer-zone and manual-handling gaps allow occasional reentry. Reject-segregation is automatic and does not add time in normal operation. Preventing rejects from re-entering flow depends on validated segregation logic more than operator skill. Rejected units reaching saleable flow risks recall-level cost, so exposure is serious though infrequent.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of accepted cartons whose product, lot, or expiry data do not match the enclosed sterile units
rows.imp_rat: Accepted cartons whose product/lot/expiry disagree with enclosed units is a core data-mismatch failure with catastrophic traceability and recall impact.
rows.sat_rat: Print inspection and code verification reliably catch most data mismatches, though reference-file setup errors still slip through. Print-and-verify systems check lot/expiry data inline in near real time. Automated OCV/OCR reduces the operator skill needed to confirm variable data. Data mismatches cause rejects and potential recalls, a significant avoided-cost driver.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartoned sterile units bearing obsolete or incorrect regulatory content
rows.imp_rat: Obsolete or incorrect regulatory content on cartoned units is a labeling-compliance failure central to release with catastrophic recall/legal consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Version control catches most obsolete artwork, but obsolete regulatory content still escapes through change-management gaps. Verifying regulatory content against current versions is often a manual pre-run step that adds time. Ensuring current regulatory content depends on document control and operator diligence rather than automation. Obsolete content forces rework or recalls, a meaningful cost when missed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of sterile-unit integrity being compromised during containment and restart
rows.imp_rat: Compromising unit integrity during containment and restart is a recovery-handling failure central to protecting product with severe sterility impact.
rows.sat_rat: Restart handling is a known weak point where integrity assurance is inconsistent. Containment and restart procedures consume unplanned time during events. Protecting integrity during restart requires experienced judgement about handling. Compromised units during restart are lost product, a recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartons containing sterile units that do not match the designated product, strength, or batch configuration
rows.imp_rat: Cartons enclosing non-matching product/strength/batch units is a core mix-up failure with catastrophic misdispensing and recall consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Scanning of unit and carton codes with reconciliation makes gross product/strength/batch mismatches rare, though setup errors remain a residual risk. Barcode/vision verification confirms product match without slowing the line materially. Automated code reading reduces reliance on operator judgement for product matching. Mismatched product cartons force costly quarantine and rework when they occur.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an incorrect carton label version is used for the current batch
rows.imp_rat: Using an incorrect carton label version is a labeling-compliance failure central to correct release with catastrophic recall/legal consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Barcode-verified label issuance reduces wrong-version use, but change control gaps still allow occasional errors. Label version verification is a pre-run manual/scanned step adding modest time. Correct version selection relies on operator and document-control diligence. Wrong label versions drive rejects and potential recalls.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of sterile units being paired with incorrect secondary packaging during recovery
rows.imp_rat: Pairing units with incorrect secondary packaging during recovery is a mix-up hazard central to correct cartoning with severe safety consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Manual recovery is error-prone and less controlled than normal running, a known weak spot. Recovery pairing during jams takes manual time to verify correctly. Correctly re-pairing units during recovery demands careful operator judgement. Incorrect recovery pairing risks rejects and rework costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartoning output with visible foreign matter on cartons or sterile-unit surfaces
rows.imp_rat: Visible foreign matter on cartons or unit surfaces is a contamination failure central to clean packaging with severe patient-safety and rejection consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Visible foreign matter is inconsistently caught by manual inspection, a recurring weak point. Foreign-matter detection is largely manual sampling, adding inspection time. Detecting foreign matter depends heavily on inspector attention. Foreign matter causes rejects and complaint costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that rejected cartons are reintroduced into accepted output before batch disposition
rows.imp_rat: Rejected cartons reintroduced into accepted output before disposition delivers defective product to market, a severe quality-system and safety failure.
rows.sat_rat: Locked reject stations and reconciliation reduce reintroduction, but manual handling leaves residual risk. Reject segregation is handled during run without added delay. Depends on operator handling of reject bins and controls. Reintroduced rejects risk costly recalls.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of sterile units inserted into cartons bearing a mismatched product, strength, lot, or market identity
rows.imp_rat: Mismatched product/strength/lot/market identity on the carton is a core traceability failure of the job with catastrophic, recall-level consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Vision systems, code readers, and reconciliation catch most mismatches, giving well-established but not perfect assurance. Verification of carton-to-unit identity match is embedded in normal run pacing without major delay. Correct matching depends on operator vigilance and setup discipline rather than exotic expertise. Mismatched cartons cause expensive rejects and rework, motivating strong controls.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of rejected or suspect sterile units reentering acceptable packaging flow
rows.imp_rat: Rejected/suspect units reentering acceptable flow reintroduces defective product into release, a severe patient-safety and quality-system failure.
rows.sat_rat: Automated reject chutes and locked reject bins make reentry uncommon but manual recovery remains a weak point. Segregation and reject-handling procedures add controlled time at reject points. Preventing reject reentry relies on disciplined operator handling and clear segregation zones. Reentry of rejected units risks recalls, so quarantine practices are enforced.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sterile units inserted into packaging designated for a different product, strength, or lot
rows.imp_rat: Inserting units into packaging for a different product, strength, or lot is a classic mix-up that is central to correct cartoning and catastrophic in consequence (misdispensing, recall).
rows.sat_rat: Mature GMP line-clearance, segregation, and label-reconciliation practices make gross wrong-product insertion rare though not impossible. Line clearance and reconciliation checks add time but are routinely built into the schedule for changeovers. Preventing cross-product mix-ups relies heavily on trained operators following strict clearance procedures and dual verification. A single mispacked lot can trigger costly full-batch rejection or recall, so the status quo invests heavily here.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that components from another product or batch are used in accepted cartons
rows.imp_rat: Components from another product or batch used in accepted cartons is a cross-contamination/mix-up failure with catastrophic safety and recall impact.
rows.sat_rat: Material reconciliation and clearance controls make wrong-component use uncommon but not impossible. Component reconciliation and line clearance add setup time to prevent wrong-component use. Correct component use relies on operator staging discipline and material accountability. Wrong components cause batch loss and investigation costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of recommending acceptance when carton erection or labeled sterile unit insertion does not meet defined suitability requirements
rows.imp_rat: Recommending acceptance when erection/insertion fails suitability defeats the accept/reject decision, letting nonconforming cartons through with severe impact.
rows.sat_rat: Defined suitability criteria and sign-off gates reduce false acceptance, yet ambiguous criteria still let marginal cases through. Acceptance decisions are gated by review cycles that add time but rarely dominate the concern here. Correctly distinguishing conforming from non-conforming performance leans heavily on evaluator expertise and interpretation. A wrong acceptance carries large downstream cost, but the assessment activity itself is not the cost driver.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of residual units or packaging materials from a prior configuration entering the next batch
rows.imp_rat: Residual prior-configuration units/materials entering the next batch is a line-clearance failure central to preventing mix-ups with catastrophic consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Documented line-clearance procedures are mature but residual-material escapes remain a recurring risk. Clearance and cleaning between batches consumes scheduled changeover time. Thorough line clearance depends on operator diligence following checklists. Residual carryover causes rejects and investigations, justifying clearance effort.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of finished cartons missing required patient information or containing incorrect patient information
rows.imp_rat: Missing or incorrect patient information on finished cartons directly endangers patients and violates labeling law; central to correct release and catastrophic.
rows.sat_rat: Print inspection catches many defects but incorrect setup or missing content can still pass if the reference is wrong. Variable-data verification runs inline without notable delay. Automated print verification lowers dependence on manual proofreading of patient information. Incorrect patient info drives rejects and potential recall exposure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of sterile units with damaged delivery components after carton insertion
rows.imp_rat: Damaged delivery components after insertion compromise product usability; central to safe insertion and severe because the device may fail to deliver dose.
rows.sat_rat: Damage during insertion is intermittent and not fully caught by standard inspection. Detecting delivery-component damage adds little inline time. Identifying damaged delivery components can require careful inspection or specialized detection. Damaged components destroy the unit's value, a meaningful loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing the sequence or disposition status of affected units
rows.imp_rat: Losing sequence or disposition status of affected units corrupts traceability and accept/reject control; moderately central with severe recall consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Manual segregation and labeling of affected units frequently loses sequence during interventions. Reconstructing lost disposition status after the fact consumes significant time. Tracking unit sequence and disposition during chaos demands disciplined attention that is hard to sustain. Lost disposition status often forces scrapping otherwise good units, a real cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of product identity, carton identity, or traceability data being carried over incorrectly between formats
rows.imp_rat: Identity/traceability data carried over incorrectly between formats is a core mix-up/traceability failure with severe recall consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Format-change data handling is partly systematized but manual transcription steps leave recurring carry-over risk. Data carry-over checks between formats are procedural and do not materially slow the assessment. Preventing incorrect carry-over depends on disciplined data management that still relies heavily on human diligence. Corrupted traceability data can invalidate batches, making the potential cost severe though intermittent.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of foreign material found on sterile units or inside cartons after packaging
rows.imp_rat: Foreign material found on units or inside cartons is a contamination failure central to clean packaging with severe patient-safety consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Good housekeeping and inspection reduce but do not eliminate foreign-material findings. Foreign-material checks are part of routine inline and cleaning practice. Preventing foreign material relies on housekeeping discipline and environmental controls. Foreign material triggers costly rejects and investigations.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that rejection status is assigned to a different labeled sterile unit than the one physically rejected
rows.imp_rat: Assigning rejection status to the wrong unit means a defective unit stays in flow while a good one is discarded, a severe disposition-integrity failure.
rows.sat_rat: Reject verification stations perform well in steady state, but timing and buffering edge cases still allow occasional mismapping. Reject tracking is automated at the reject station and adds no meaningful time. Correct reject-to-identity mapping relies on validated station logic rather than operator skill in normal cases. A misassigned rejection can let bad product flow or scrap good product, a costly but not everyday event.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that packaging configurations permit product or label mix-ups
rows.imp_rat: Packaging configurations that permit product/label mix-ups is a design-level hazard moderately central to the job with severe mislabeling consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Poka-yoke and segregation practices are established but mix-up risk from similar configurations is never fully eliminated. Verifying that configurations prevent mix-ups requires deliberate design review adding to schedule. Anticipating mix-up pathways needs human-factors and packaging expertise that is not universal. A mix-up reaching market is extremely costly, dwarfing prevention effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that preceding-batch labeling materials are mistaken for current-batch materials
rows.imp_rat: Mistaking preceding-batch labeling materials for current is a line-clearance mix-up failure central to correct labeling with severe recall consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Formal clearance reduces prior-batch mix-ups but they remain a known human-dependent risk. Line clearance between batches adds time to prevent material mix-ups. Relies on operator discipline to segregate prior-batch materials. Prior-batch material use causes costly batch loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that incorrect sterile-unit count, position, or orientation escapes verification
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect count, position, or orientation escaping verification defeats the inspection gate for insertion quality, a severe escape reaching patients.
rows.sat_rat: Inspection systems handle count and position well but orientation subtleties still escape at times. Automated count/position checks minimize added verification time. Configuring inspection correctly for position and orientation needs skill. Escaped count/orientation errors drive rejects and complaints.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of mismatches among sterile-unit identity, carton identity, and traceability records
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch among unit identity, carton identity, and traceability records is a core data-integrity failure with severe recall and compliance impact.
rows.sat_rat: Serialization aggregation links identities well, though gaps between physical unit and record still occur. Reconciling identities across records is largely automated but exception handling takes time. Traceability reconciliation depends on system configuration expertise. Mismatches cause hold and investigation costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of batches containing components from different approved cartoning configurations
rows.imp_rat: Batches containing components from different approved configurations is a mix-up/line-clearance failure central to the job with severe recall consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Clearance and reconciliation make mixed configurations uncommon but human-dependent. Preventing mixed configurations relies on line clearance which adds setup time. Depends on operator discipline in staging and clearance. Mixed-configuration batches risk costly rejection.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that patient-safety or regulatory constraints are omitted from packaging trade-offs
rows.imp_rat: Omitting patient-safety or regulatory constraints from trade-offs is an upstream criteria gap; moderately central but severe because it can green-light unsafe configurations.
rows.sat_rat: Trade-off decisions still rely on individuals remembering constraints, so omissions occur despite formal risk processes. Ensuring safety and regulatory constraints enter every trade-off adds review time that is often compressed under deadlines. Recognizing which patient-safety and regulatory constraints apply demands scarce cross-disciplinary regulatory judgement. Omitting a constraint can be inexpensive to avoid yet catastrophically costly in recall or non-compliance.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that incorrect carton identity, lot, or expiry data passes setup verification
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect carton identity/lot/expiry passing setup verification defeats the verification gate; central to correct release with severe recall consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Vision systems and print inspection widely deployed catch most errors, but setup-phase verification still admits occasional escapes. Verifying variable print data at setup takes time but is routine. Requires careful reading and comparison, modest skill demand. Wrong lot/expiry data escaping causes recalls, a severe cost exposure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartons with invalid, duplicated, or mismatched traceability identifiers
rows.imp_rat: Invalid, duplicated, or mismatched traceability identifiers is a serialization-integrity failure central to the job with severe compliance and recall consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Serialization reliably rejects duplicates and invalid codes, with residual mismatch cases. Serialization systems flag duplicate/invalid IDs inline. System configuration expertise underlies reliable ID checking. Bad identifiers cause reconciliation cost and holds.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that packaging geometry exposes sterile units to insertion damage
rows.imp_rat: Packaging geometry that exposes units to insertion damage is a design factor central to safe insertion with severe sterility consequences when it occurs.
rows.sat_rat: Design reviews catch many geometry issues but subtle exposure paths still slip through to production. Assessing geometry-driven insertion exposure needs iterative prototyping and testing that extends timelines. Predicting how geometry exposes units to damage requires experienced packaging-design intuition. Geometry errors caught late force tooling rework and scrap, a significant cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of cartons containing a missing, duplicated, or incorrectly oriented labeled sterile unit
rows.imp_rat: Missing, duplicated, or misoriented units in cartons is a core insertion-correctness failure with severe supply and safety consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Presence/count sensors and reject systems catch most defects, though orientation errors and sensor drift leave gaps. Inline detection avoids added time when working correctly. Setting up detection sensors correctly requires trained technicians. Missing or duplicated units cause rejects and material loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartons whose lot, expiry, or serialization data disagree with the packaged sterile unit
rows.imp_rat: Lot/expiry/serialization data disagreeing with the packaged unit is a core data-integrity failure with severe traceability and recall consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Serialization and code verification reliably catch most disagreements, though aggregation and grading errors persist. Serialization/aggregation checks run inline in near real time. Serialization systems reduce the manual skill needed to reconcile codes. Data disagreements can halt lines and cause costly reconciliation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartons with compromised closures or structural damage after packaging
rows.imp_rat: Compromised closures or structural damage after packaging defeats the protective/closure job with severe integrity and safety consequences.
rows.sat_rat: End-of-line inspection reliably catches most structural damage. Closure/structural checks are performed inline with negligible delay. Detecting damaged cartons relies on operator attention and end-of-line inspection. Damaged cartons cause rejects and rework but are relatively cheap to detect.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartons with incorrect, incomplete, or unreadable required label information
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect, incomplete, or unreadable required label information is a labeling-compliance failure central to release with severe regulatory consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Vision inspection reliably catches most illegible or incomplete labels, though edge cases escape. Inline OCR/vision verifies label readability without slowing throughput much. Automated verification lowers reliance on manual proofreading. Unreadable labels cause rejects and rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a sterile unit is assigned to the wrong parent carton in the specified hierarchy
rows.imp_rat: Assigning a unit to the wrong parent carton in the hierarchy corrupts aggregation/traceability, central to serialized packaging with severe compliance impact.
rows.sat_rat: Deployed serialization-aggregation practice reliably enforces hierarchy in normal running, though jams and manual interventions remain weak points. Verification of correct parent assignment is handled inline by vision and aggregation systems without appreciable added time. Established aggregation logic offloads most correctness decisions from operator judgement to the machine's serialization station. Misassignment triggers expensive quarantine and rework, but modern systems keep these events rare enough that routine cost is contained.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of sterile units being dropped or ejected during transfer
rows.imp_rat: Units dropped or ejected during transfer risks damage/loss and misfeed; central to reliable insertion with severe integrity consequences when it occurs.
rows.sat_rat: Grippers and guides reduce drops but transfer at speed still ejects units inconsistently. Dropped units are noticed instantly but cannot be prevented in the moment. Minimizing drops depends on well-tuned transfer setup by skilled technicians. Dropped or ejected units are scrapped, a steady material loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that intermittent carton erection or labeled sterile unit insertion failures remain undetected during qualification
rows.imp_rat: Intermittent failures undetected during qualification undermine validation confidence; moderately central and severe if latent failures reach production.
rows.sat_rat: Intermittent faults are notoriously hard to catch and standard protocols frequently miss them, leaving a real detection gap. Detecting intermittent failures requires extended running, which lengthens qualification but is not the core loss. Spotting intermittent faults demands vigilant, experienced observers who know the failure signatures. Extra run time and scrapped units add cost when chasing intermittent issues.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cartons whose identity or expiry information conflicts with the enclosed sterile unit
rows.imp_rat: Carton identity/expiry conflicting with the enclosed unit is a core data-integrity failure with severe misdispensing and recall consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Code reconciliation and data checks make conflicting identity/expiry relatively uncommon. Verifying expiry/identity coherence adds little time when automated code checks run. Reconciling carton identity with enclosed unit depends on setup discipline and system controls. Conflicting identity/expiry info causes expensive holds and potential recalls.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartons containing an incorrect sterile-unit count
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect sterile-unit count is a core content-correctness failure with severe dosing/supply and regulatory consequences (near-duplicate of quantity errors).
rows.sat_rat: Checkweighing and unit counting reliably detect miscounts, making incorrect-count cartons uncommon. Count verification is automated inline with no meaningful delay. Sensors and checkweighers reduce reliance on operator counting. Incorrect counts cause rejects but the detection cost is low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that configured rejection criteria differ from approved requirements for the scheduled carton format
rows.imp_rat: Configured rejection criteria differing from approved requirements weakens the reject gate; central to correct disposition with severe escape consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe-driven reject configuration helps, but manual overrides create mismatches with approved requirements. Configuring rejection criteria correctly is a brief setup step. Requires understanding of approved reject logic and settings. Wrong criteria cause good rejects or bad accepts, both costly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cartons containing an incorrect sterile-unit quantity
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect sterile-unit quantity in a carton is a core content-correctness failure with severe dosing/supply and recall consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Count-detection sensors and end-of-line checkweighers reliably catch most quantity errors. Count verification is handled inline by machine sensors with negligible added time. Correct quantity depends on machine setup and sensor function more than operator skill. Incorrect counts cause rejects and complaints, justifying inline count controls.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartons with coding or label content that does not match the approved configuration
rows.imp_rat: Coding or label content not matching approved configuration is a labeling-integrity failure central to correct release with severe regulatory/recall consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Automated code-inspection and first-off checks make label-content mismatches well controlled. Code and label content checks occur at start-up and periodically without significant time burden. Verification of coding relies on operator checks and increasingly automated code inspection. Incorrect coding leads to costly holds and rework, so verification is prioritized.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that carton dimensions force or bind sterile units during insertion
rows.imp_rat: Carton dimensions forcing or binding units during insertion is a fit failure central to safe insertion, severe because binding can damage containers.
rows.sat_rat: Correct tooling largely prevents binding, but dimensional variation still forces units inconsistently. Binding during insertion causes immediate jams and stoppages. Preventing bind depends on correct format-part selection by setup techs. Forced insertion damages units and cartons, a recurring loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cartons with erection, labeling, or insertion deviations that pass unrecognized
rows.imp_rat: Erection/labeling/insertion deviations passing unrecognized is a detection escape moderately central to release with severe nonconformance consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Inspection reduces but does not eliminate deviations that pass unrecognized, a recurring pain. Unrecognized deviations by definition are not caught in time. Recognizing subtle erection/labeling/insertion deviations depends heavily on operator experience. Unrecognized deviations lead to downstream scrap and investigation costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartons with sterile-unit quantities or component arrangements outside the defined secondary configuration
rows.imp_rat: Quantities or arrangements outside the defined secondary configuration is a content-correctness failure central to the job with severe nonconformance consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Setup validation and inspection make configuration errors uncommon, though arrangement defects still slip. Configuration verification is largely inline with modest delay. Correct configuration setup relies on skilled changeover technicians. Out-of-configuration cartons cause rejects and rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of sterile units being misoriented or incompletely seated within cartons
rows.imp_rat: Misoriented or incompletely seated units is a direct insertion-quality failure central to the CFJ, severe due to potential damage and nonconformance.
rows.sat_rat: Guides, sensors, and reject stations handle most misseating but occasional escapes persist. Seating verification is inline and does not add meaningful time. Proper seating relies on machine setup and format tooling rather than operator skill. Misoriented units cause rejects and jams that waste material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical sterile-product packaging requirements omitted from evaluation criteria
rows.imp_rat: Omitting critical sterile-product requirements from evaluation criteria means defects go unchecked; moderately central but severe because escaping defects reach patients.
rows.sat_rat: Requirement capture today depends on human recall and fragmented sources, so critical omissions recur despite review gates. Assembling complete sterile-product requirement sets pulls from many documents and takes considerable elapsed time. Knowing which sterile-packaging requirements are critical enough to include leans heavily on regulatory and QA expertise that few individuals hold. Omitted critical requirements are inexpensive to prevent but very costly when they force late redesign.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartons left partially open or insecurely closed after cartoning
rows.imp_rat: Partially open or insecurely closed cartons is a direct closure failure central to protection with severe integrity and sterility-exposure consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Closure sensors reliably detect open/insecure cartons, with rare escapes. Open-flap detection is inline. Automated sensing reduces operator dependence. Insecure closures cause rejects and complaints.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cartons missing required label content
rows.imp_rat: Cartons missing required label content is a labeling-compliance failure central to release with severe regulatory and patient-safety consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Vision reliably detects missing content when the reference is correct, though reference errors escape. Content verification is inline via OCR/vision. Automated content checks reduce manual proofreading load. Missing label content causes rejects and recall risk.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the specified carton arrangement damages a sterile unit or its label during insertion
rows.imp_rat: A carton arrangement that damages a unit or label during insertion threatens integrity; relevant to safe insertion and severe when it breaches product.
rows.sat_rat: Mature cartoner tooling handles gentle insertion reliably for validated formats, with damage mainly on marginal or new configurations. Insertion damage checks are handled during setup qualification without ongoing time penalty. Mechanical tooling and format parts are engineered by specialists, reducing reliance on operator finesse during running. Damaged units and labels are discarded, but well-designed insertion keeps this loss low in steady state.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that serialization criteria allow insertion conditions that compromise sterile-barrier integrity
rows.imp_rat: If serialization criteria permit sterility-compromising insertion conditions, it indirectly threatens the barrier; moderately relevant but severe in outcome.
rows.sat_rat: Serialization and sterility disciplines are often specified separately, so conflicting conditions are caught inconsistently rather than by a robust integrated practice. Checking that serialization criteria don't force barrier-compromising insertion is done during design reviews without significant time cost. Bridging serialization requirements with sterile-barrier constraints demands scarce cross-disciplinary expertise that the status quo does not fully supply. A barrier compromise reaching product is extremely costly, so latent exposure is high even if occurrences are infrequent.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of sustained compression on sterile units during machine stoppages
rows.imp_rat: Sustained compression during stoppages can crush primary containers; moderately relevant to handling protection and severe due to potential sterility breach.
rows.sat_rat: Few machines automatically relieve compression on stop, so outcomes depend on chance duration of the stoppage. Sustained compression accrues silently during stoppages with no timer prompting operators to relieve it. Recognizing that stopped units are being crushed requires attentive judgement not consistently applied. Crushed sterile units during stoppages become scrap, a real but intermittent loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of using an obsolete specification revision to configure the next carton format
rows.imp_rat: Using an obsolete spec revision to configure a format is a configuration-control failure central to correct output with severe compliance consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Document control systems largely prevent obsolete-revision use, though manual copies still slip through. Checking revision currency is quick when document control works. Low skill—mostly a matter of verifying revision status. Using an obsolete revision can scrap a whole run.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartons crushed, torn, or deformed during cartoning
rows.imp_rat: Cartons crushed, torn, or deformed during cartoning is a direct handling-quality failure central to protection with severe integrity consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Vision and ejection reliably remove deformed cartons, though machine wear causes intermittent damage. Damaged-carton detection is inline. Machine setup skill reduces mechanical damage rates. Crushed/torn cartons are scrapped, a modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of accepted cartons that are incompletely erected, unclosed, crushed, or torn
rows.imp_rat: Incompletely erected, unclosed, crushed, or torn accepted cartons is a core erection/closure failure with severe protection and appearance consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Erection and closure inspection reliably rejects most defective cartons, though borderline defects occasionally pass. Vision and ejection handle crushed/torn cartons inline immediately. Detection is automated, reducing operator dependence. Rejected cartons and blanks are scrapped, a modest recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartons with illegible label text or machine-readable content
rows.imp_rat: Illegible label text or machine-readable content breaks identification/traceability; central to compliant release with severe recall and dispensing consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Code grading and OCV reliably catch illegible content, making this well controlled. Barcode grading and OCR run inline instantly. Automated grading removes reliance on operator readability judgement. Illegible codes cause rejects and downstream scanning failures with cost impact.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a closed carton reopens during downstream handling
rows.imp_rat: A closed carton reopening downstream defeats the closure job's purpose, exposing units with severe integrity and sterility consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Hot-melt glue and tuck-lock closures are mature and hold well when set correctly, with occasional pop-open failures. Reopened cartons are found downstream, causing rework delays. Reliable closure depends on correct glue/tuck setup by skilled staff. Reopened cartons cause rework and rejects, moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of restarting cartoning before the interrupting condition is fully corrected
rows.imp_rat: Restarting before the condition is corrected risks propagating defects/damage; moderately central to safe recovery and severe in potential consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Restart interlocks help but operators can override or misjudge readiness, so premature restarts still occur. Verifying full correction before restart delays resuming production. Judging whether a condition is fully corrected before restart requires experienced judgement. Premature restart scraps more material, an avoidable recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that serialization criteria permit duplicate sterile-unit identifiers within the identification scope
rows.imp_rat: Serialization criteria permitting duplicate identifiers corrupts unique traceability; moderately central and severe due to compliance and recall-tracking failure.
rows.sat_rat: Uniqueness enforcement within an identification scope is a mature, well-solved capability of serialization systems. Duplicate-prevention is enforced at number generation with no runtime cost. Serialization number management systems handle uniqueness automatically, requiring minimal user skill. Duplicate identifiers would cause costly data conflicts, but systems make this rare.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of erecting a carton format that does not match the batch specification
rows.imp_rat: Erecting a format not matching the batch spec is a wrong-configuration failure central to correct packaging with severe compliance consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Setup checklists and format-part controls make format verification fairly consistent, though human verification still fails occasionally. Wrong-format detection at startup is quick when checklists are followed. Verifying carton format against batch spec relies on operator attention and reading skill. Wrong-format runs waste materials and time, but are usually caught early.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required material, dimensional, closure, or tamper-evidence attributes missing from carton specifications
rows.imp_rat: Missing material/dimensional/closure/tamper attributes from specs is an upstream completeness gap moderately central to the job, severe if tamper-evidence is lost.
rows.sat_rat: Spec templates capture most attributes, yet gaps in closure or tamper-evidence detail still recur. Compiling complete carton attribute specifications is a documentation-heavy task that takes real effort. Knowing every required material, dimensional, closure, and tamper-evidence attribute demands specification expertise. Missing attributes trigger rework and delay but the specification effort itself is low cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of misalignment between criterion weighting and patient-safety and compliance risks
rows.imp_rat: Misaligned criterion weighting versus safety/compliance risks is an upstream evaluation issue peripheral to running the machine but severe if it deprioritizes safety.
rows.sat_rat: Weighting stays subjective and inconsistent, so alignment to actual risk is unreliable. Aligning criterion weights to risk requires risk-workshop time that is often abbreviated. Correctly weighting criteria against patient-safety and compliance risk demands scarce risk-analysis judgement. Misweighting has no direct material cost but drives poor selection decisions.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in acceptance criteria for erection, insertion, closure, and labeling
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in acceptance criteria for the key steps undermines consistent judgment; moderately central and severe if it causes wrong accept/reject decisions.
rows.sat_rat: Ambiguity leads to inconsistent acceptance decisions that undermine repeatable outcomes. Drafting unambiguous acceptance criteria across four operations is a slow, iterative wording exercise. Writing crisp, testable criteria requires rare technical-writing plus process expertise, and ambiguity persists where that skill is thin. Ambiguous criteria cause disputes and rework rather than direct material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of accepted cartons with missing or incorrect secondary components
rows.imp_rat: Accepted cartons with missing or incorrect secondary components is a content-completeness escape with severe compliance and safety consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Detection is reliable for presence but incorrect-component identity is harder to catch, leaving a residual gap. Inline presence/verification checks add little delay. Automation reduces dependence on operator verification of components. Missing/incorrect components drive rejects and rework costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of mismatch between expected quality states and the active secondary-package configuration
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between expected quality states and active configuration risks wrong disposition; moderately central and severe if nonconforming product is accepted.
rows.sat_rat: State-configuration alignment is partly manual and error-prone, a recurring gap. Reconciling quality states with active configuration is often manual and lagging. Requires QA judgement to match states to configurations. Mismatches cause holds and rework costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cartons missing required secondary components
rows.imp_rat: Missing required secondary components is a completeness failure central to correct secondary packaging with severe compliance/usability consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Presence detection reliably catches most missing components, with occasional undetected omissions. Missing-component detection via sensors is inline and fast. Sensor presence checks reduce reliance on manual verification. Missing secondary components cause rejects and rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of carton-derived particulate released during erection
rows.imp_rat: Carton-derived particulate during erection is a contamination risk to sterile units; moderately central to clean cartoning and severe if particulate reaches product.
rows.sat_rat: Status quo relies on enclosure guarding and vacuum extraction that only partly control shedding, so contamination risk remains an inconsistent, unsolved pain. Particulate release happens continuously during erection and cannot be quickly addressed once cartons run, so operators tolerate it in real time. Reducing carton dust depends largely on carton-blank quality and machine tooling rather than operator skill, leaving little the executor can do. Chronic particulate drives cleaning cycles and occasional rejected units, a persistent low-level material and labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of discrepancies between registered market configurations and approved production specifications
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancies between registered market configs and approved specs risk wrong-market release; moderately central and severe due to regulatory consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Document control catches many discrepancies but cross-market spec conflicts persist. Reconciling registered vs approved specs is a periodic manual review. Requires regulatory/QA expertise to catch discrepancies. Spec discrepancies risk costly compliance failures.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that sterile-unit identifiers become unreadable during insertion or carton erection
rows.imp_rat: Unreadable identifiers after insertion/erection break traceability; moderately central and severe because they impede serialization and recall capability.
rows.sat_rat: Inline print-and-verify with grade cameras is a mature standard practice that keeps unreadable identifiers uncommon in production. Readability verification is done inline by vision systems without slowing the line. Automated code-grading offloads the judgement of readability from operators. Unreadable identifiers cause rejects and rework, but grading catches most before they propagate, limiting cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of labeled sterile units inserted in the wrong position or orientation while flow is established
rows.imp_rat: Units inserted in wrong position/orientation while flow is established is an insertion-quality failure central to the CFJ with severe usability consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Tooling constrains orientation but early-flow mispositioning is frequent before stabilization. Correcting insertion orientation while establishing flow costs setup time. Getting position/orientation right during flow establishment needs finesse. Wrong-position inserts scrap trial units.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartons with missing, incorrect, or extra configuration components
rows.imp_rat: Missing, incorrect, or extra configuration components is a content-completeness failure central to correct secondary packaging with severe compliance impact.
rows.sat_rat: Inline detection of missing leaflets/components is common but occasional escapes still occur. Component presence checks are integrated into the run with minimal delay. Ensuring all inserts and components are present requires operator attention during setup and running. Missing or extra components trigger rework and material loss, driving inspection effort.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartons providing inadequate sterile-unit clearance or restraint
rows.imp_rat: Inadequate clearance or restraint risks unit movement/damage in transit; moderately central to fit correctness and severe if it leads to damage.
rows.sat_rat: Dimensional checks and samples catch most clearance issues but tolerance stack-ups still produce inadequate cartons at times. Checking clearance and restraint across carton designs requires fit trials that take time to complete. Specifying adequate clearance and restraint draws on dimensional and materials expertise. Inadequate clearance leads to rejected cartons and rework, a recurring material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between evaluation criteria and required sterile-unit, carton, label, and secondary-packaging configurations
rows.imp_rat: Mismatched evaluation criteria against required configurations means verification checks the wrong things; moderately central to the job and severe because bad units can be accepted.
rows.sat_rat: Status quo relies on checklists and expert review that still leave criteria misaligned with actual configurations often enough to cause downstream surprises. Reconciling evaluation criteria against every configuration variant is a slow, manual cross-referencing exercise that lengthens the specification cycle. Catching mismatches demands seasoned packaging engineers who deeply understand sterile-unit, carton, and label interactions, a scarce expertise. Missed mismatches surface late as requalification and rework, but the direct evaluation activity itself is relatively cheap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartons with erection or closure characteristics outside defined tolerances
rows.imp_rat: Erection or closure characteristics outside tolerance is a direct quality failure of the core sub-jobs with severe protection consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Inspection catches many tolerance excursions but subtle drift within limits is under-monitored. Tolerance checks on erection/closure are inline where instrumented. Interpreting tolerance drift relies on technician judgement. Out-of-tolerance cartons cause rejects.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of rejected output from different runs becoming commingled before disposition
rows.imp_rat: Commingling rejected output from different runs before disposition confuses reconciliation; moderately central and severe for traceability/disposition integrity.
rows.sat_rat: Reject bins and labeling exist but manual segregation of rejects across runs is inconsistently enforced. Keeping rejects from different runs separated adds handling time. Requires disciplined labeling and quarantine habits that vary by operator. Commingled rejects can force broader disposition scrap, a real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of sterile-unit exposure to handling conditions outside approved limits during cartoning
rows.imp_rat: Exposure to handling conditions outside approved limits threatens stability; moderately central and severe if it degrades the sterile product.
rows.sat_rat: Exposure limits are monitored but jams and slowdowns can extend exposure unpredictably. Limiting exposure time depends on line speed and prompt handling that are only partly controlled. Managing exposure duration relies on operator pacing and awareness of limits. Excess exposure can spoil units, an avoidable loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartons using a format that does not match the approved packaging configuration
rows.imp_rat: Wrong carton format versus approved configuration undermines correct secondary packaging; relevant to the job and severe if released nonconforming.
rows.sat_rat: Setup verification against approved specs is a mature control that reliably catches format errors. Confirming approved format at setup is a routine, time-bounded step. Format selection follows documented setup sheets so moderate skill suffices. Wrong-format cartons waste material and cause rework but are usually caught at setup.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of erecting a carton blank that does not match the current sterile-unit format
rows.imp_rat: Erecting a blank not matching the current format disrupts the core erection job and can cause damage or wrong packaging, a serious but often setup-caught error.
rows.sat_rat: Barcode/format checks and job specifications catch most mismatches but visually similar blanks still slip through occasionally. Format verification adds setup time but is a routine gate, not a bottleneck. Correct blank-to-format matching still leans on operator attention and recognition of subtle carton differences. A mismatched blank run wastes materials and forces rework if caught late.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of erection, insertion, closure, or labeling criteria with undefined acceptance thresholds
rows.imp_rat: Undefined acceptance thresholds is a criteria-completeness gap moderately central to consistent judgment, with moderate consequences that setup usually surfaces.
rows.sat_rat: Many criteria still lack quantified thresholds, so pass/fail judgements vary and outcomes are inconsistent. Defining acceptance thresholds for every criterion requires data-gathering and negotiation that lengthens setup. Setting defensible thresholds needs statistical and process knowledge held by few. Undefined thresholds cause inspection disputes and rework rather than raw material loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartons that fail erection or closure specifications
rows.imp_rat: Cartons failing erection or closure specs is a direct quality failure of the erection/closure sub-job with severe protection consequences when released.
rows.sat_rat: Erection/closure inspection reliably catches failures, though marginal closures occasionally pass. Closure inspection is inline and immediate. Detection is automated; setup skill affects rates. Failed closures scrap cartons and product.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of conflict between approved carton specifications and cartoning-machine setup instructions
rows.imp_rat: Conflict between approved specs and setup instructions is an upstream configuration-integrity issue; moderately central and severe if it drives wrong setup.
rows.sat_rat: Approved setup sheets usually align with specs but stale or conflicting documents still appear. Resolving spec-versus-instruction conflicts stalls setup. Detecting and reconciling conflicts requires experienced judgement. Conflicts cause rework and wasted materials.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cartoning-machine settings configured outside specified tolerances for the next carton format
rows.imp_rat: Machine settings configured outside tolerance for the next format risks defective erection/insertion; moderately central and severe if it damages product.
rows.sat_rat: Setup sheets and digital setpoint recall improve consistency, but manual-adjust machines still drift out of tolerance. Bringing settings within tolerance can take iterative trials. Dialing settings to tolerance is skill-intensive adjustment work. Out-of-tolerance settings produce scrap trial cartons.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that setup verification uses cartons, labels, leaflets, or sterile units from the wrong format
rows.imp_rat: Verifying setup with wrong-format materials invalidates the setup check; moderately central and severe because it can approve an incorrect configuration.
rows.sat_rat: Line clearance procedures reduce this but wrong-format samples occasionally enter verification. Confirming verification uses right-format samples adds a check step. Depends on disciplined material control rather than expertise. Using wrong-format samples invalidates verification and wastes materials.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of failing to escalate an interruption outside routine correction limits
rows.imp_rat: Failing to escalate an interruption beyond routine limits is a process-control gap moderately relevant to the job, severe if it allows uncontrolled recovery.
rows.sat_rat: SOP escalation triggers exist but depend on operator recognition, so escalation is inconsistently applied. Escalation timing suffers when operators hesitate, delaying proper response. Knowing when an interruption exceeds routine limits requires trained judgement that varies among operators. Failure to escalate can compound into larger losses, an indirect but real cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that installed change parts do not match the approved carton format
rows.imp_rat: Change parts not matching approved carton format is a setup error affecting erection quality; moderately relevant and severe because it can yield nonconforming cartons.
rows.sat_rat: Part labeling, kitting and checklists reduce mismatches but human misidentification remains a recurring gap. Verifying installed change parts against the approved format takes measurable setup time. Recognizing correct change parts depends heavily on operator familiarity and labeling discipline. Wrong parts cause scrap and downtime when discovered during trials.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of completed packaging records being unavailable for review
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable packaging records is a records/compliance gap peripheral to physical cartoning but severe because it can block release or trigger findings.
rows.sat_rat: Established EBR and paper batch-record practices make records reliably retrievable, with occasional gaps from manual entry. Records are usually available promptly when electronic batch systems are in place. Maintaining complete records requires trained documentation discipline from operators. Missing records cause costly batch holds and rework of documentation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of personnel exposure to hazardous machine motion during correction
rows.imp_rat: Personnel exposure to hazardous motion during correction is peripheral to the packaging output but severe because of serious injury potential.
rows.sat_rat: Interlocked guards and stop functions reliably limit hazardous-motion exposure during correction. Safe correction procedures add some time but are standard. Following guarded-correction rules is trainable and not expert-dependent. Personnel injuries are costly but infrequent with current controls.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in parent-child assignment rules for labeled sterile units
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in parent-child assignment rules undermines correct aggregation; moderately central and severe because it can corrupt serialization hierarchies.
rows.sat_rat: Standard templates cover common hierarchies well, yet ambiguity persists for partial and mixed configurations, leaving recurring gaps. Clarifying parent-child rules is a one-time specification activity that seldom delays throughput. Interpreting hierarchy rules for atypical configurations still leans on experienced packaging engineers, limiting how much the status quo de-skills this. Ambiguous rules discovered late force respecification and validation cost, a recurring but manageable expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of incompatibility between serialization criteria and cartoning-machine insertion, carton-erection, and sequencing constraints
rows.imp_rat: Incompatibility between serialization criteria and machine constraints is a configuration-fit issue; moderately relevant and severe if it forces bad insertion or halts serialization.
rows.sat_rat: There is no standard integrated check, so incompatibilities recur and are handled reactively rather than prevented. Reconciling serialization criteria with machine insertion and sequencing constraints often surfaces late, adding rework cycles. It requires simultaneous fluency in serialization schemes and cartoner mechanics, a rare combination the status quo relies on individuals to hold. Late-discovered incompatibilities force re-engineering and re-validation, a notable recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of operator exposure to uncontrolled motion or stored energy during changeover
rows.imp_rat: Operator exposure to uncontrolled motion/stored energy during changeover is peripheral to the packaging job itself but severe due to serious personnel-injury risk.
rows.sat_rat: Guarding, interlocks and LOTO are mature and widely deployed, making exposure to stored energy reliably controlled. Lockout procedures add time to changeover but are routinely applied. Following energy-isolation steps is standardized and trainable rather than expert-dependent. Safety incidents are costly but rare under established procedures.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartons with label placement, condition, or adhesion outside defined tolerances
rows.imp_rat: Label placement, condition, or adhesion outside tolerance is a labeling-quality issue moderately central to release with severe consequences if labels detach or become illegible.
rows.sat_rat: Vision catches placement well but adhesion/condition defects are harder to detect consistently. Adhesion and placement checks are inline where equipped. Detecting adhesion/condition issues still partly depends on operator observation. Poor adhesion causes rejects and field complaints.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in the required association between each labeled sterile unit and its carton identity
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in unit-to-carton identity association undermines correct pairing; moderately relevant and severe since it can propagate into mislabeling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard parent-child aggregation practice covers most cases well, but residual ambiguity in edge cases still slips through and remains a recurring source of misassociation. Defining unit-to-carton association rules is largely front-loaded during specification and does not add ongoing delay to the recommendation work. Mature serialization/aggregation frameworks (GS1, EPCIS) give the influencer standardized concepts, reducing the interpretive skill needed to specify these associations. Ambiguity that survives into production drives costly rework and deviation investigations, so the status quo carries real latent cost exposure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which carton labeling falls outside approved placement boundaries
rows.imp_rat: Labeling outside approved placement boundaries is a labeling-quality issue moderately central to compliant release with severe consequences if legibility/regulatory breach results.
rows.sat_rat: Vision systems reliably detect placement outside tolerance, though marginal cases persist. Vision placement checks run inline without slowing throughput. Automated placement inspection lowers reliance on visual operator judgement. Out-of-boundary labels cause rejects and rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the specified unit quantity exceeds the usable capacity of an erected carton
rows.imp_rat: Specified quantity exceeding usable carton capacity forces overfill/damage; moderately central to fit correctness and severe if it damages units.
rows.sat_rat: Physical capacity limits are well understood and routinely verified, making overcapacity errors uncommon. Capacity checks are quick geometric comparisons done during specification. Comparing unit count to carton capacity is straightforward and needs little specialist skill. Overpacking errors caught early cost little; if missed they cause scrap, but they are usually caught.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation in closure alignment from specification
rows.imp_rat: Closure alignment deviation affects carton closure quality; relevant to a job sub-step with moderate consequences unless it reaches integrity loss.
rows.sat_rat: Vision and mechanical guides make closure alignment fairly consistent on well-maintained machines, but drift over a run still produces defects. Closure misalignment is usually caught at inspection stations without major time loss. Correct closure setup requires skilled changeover technicians to dial in flap folders and glue. Misaligned closures cause rejected cartons and rework, a moderate recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of synchronization mismatch among carton erection, sterile-unit insertion, and carton closure during setup
rows.imp_rat: Synchronization mismatch among erection/insertion/closure at setup risks misinsertion; moderately central with moderate consequences usually caught before production.
rows.sat_rat: Servo phasing and cam timing give repeatable sync once set, though setup-phase mismatch is common initially. Achieving synchronization across stations can consume significant setup time. Phasing erection, insertion and closure is highly skill-dependent tuning. Sync mismatch during setup wastes trial materials.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of carton blanks outside dimensional or material specifications at cartoning
rows.imp_rat: Out-of-spec carton blanks at cartoning cause erection/closure defects; moderately central to quality with moderate consequences usually caught at erection.
rows.sat_rat: Incoming inspection catches gross defects but subtle dimensional variation still reaches the machine. Incoming material inspection is a receiving-stage step, not inline. Depends on QC sampling skill for dimensional checks. Out-of-spec blanks cause jams and scrap costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of misidentifying the condition interrupting carton flow
rows.imp_rat: Misidentifying the interrupting condition is a diagnostic ambiguity moderately relevant to correct recovery, with moderate consequences unless it drives unsafe restart.
rows.sat_rat: Basic fault codes help but many conditions still require judgement, making identification inconsistent. Misidentifying the interrupting condition prolongs diagnosis and downtime. Correctly diagnosing what stopped carton flow depends heavily on operator experience. Misdiagnosis wastes labor and can scrap material during wrong corrections.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation in erected-carton geometry from the current carton specification
rows.imp_rat: Erected-carton geometry deviation affects carton quality; relevant to the erection sub-job with moderate consequences unless it drives closure or insertion failure.
rows.sat_rat: Adjustable guides and forming plates produce consistent geometry once set, but drift and blank variation cause deviations. Achieving square geometry can require multiple timing adjustments. Getting erected-carton geometry to spec is a core skilled adjustment task. Out-of-spec geometry scraps cartons during trials.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of uncertainty that observed cartoning results are repeatable across the intended operating envelope
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty that results are repeatable across the operating envelope is a qualification-confidence concern; moderately relevant and moderately severe for validation.
rows.sat_rat: Standard DOE and repeatability protocols exist but envelope edges are routinely under-sampled, leaving genuine uncertainty about repeatability. Establishing repeatability across an operating envelope requires many runs that stretch qualification timelines, and today this is a slow, iterative effort. Judging whether observed results generalize across the envelope demands seasoned validation engineers who can design representative run matrices. Extensive repeat runs consume machine time, materials, and sterile units, making envelope coverage expensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of synchronization loss between carton transport and sterile-unit insertion
rows.imp_rat: Synchronization loss between transport and insertion risks misfeed/misinsertion; moderately central to reliable insertion with moderate consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Encoder-based synchronization is reliable in steady state but occasional loss persists. Sync loss during running forces stoppages, costing time. Restoring transport-insertion sync requires trained intervention. Lost sync jams and scraps materials.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required change parts do not fit the intended carton format
rows.imp_rat: Change parts not fitting the intended format is largely a setup fit issue; moderately relevant and typically caught before running, with moderate severity.
