rows.sat_rat: Dedicated change-part kits per format usually ensure fit, though wear and mixed kits cause intermittent problems. Discovering a part does not fit mid-setup causes disruptive delays. Judging fit requires experienced operators who know the machine and format tolerances. Ill-fitting parts risk damage to parts or machine and wasted setup effort.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required cartoning-machine settings with missing or conflicting values for the next carton format
rows.imp_rat: Missing or conflicting setting values is a setup-data completeness gap moderately relevant to correct configuration, with moderate consequences typically caught at setup.
rows.sat_rat: Complete parameter records reduce gaps but conflicting or missing values still surface on older formats. Chasing missing setting values delays completion of setup. Filling gaps in setting values relies on operator experience. Guessed values cause scrap and rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of variation in erected-carton position at transfer to sterile-unit insertion
rows.imp_rat: Variation in erected-carton position at transfer risks misinsertion; moderately relevant to reliable insertion with moderate consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Guides and servo transfers hold position well but variation from blank quality remains. Reducing transfer-position variation takes iterative tuning time. Controlling erected-carton position at transfer is skilled adjustment. Position variation causes mis-inserts and scrap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of synchronization failures between carton movement and sterile-unit arrival during startup
rows.imp_rat: Synchronization failures at startup risk misinsertion or jams; moderately relevant to reliable operation with moderate, largely recoverable consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Modern electronic camming reduces startup sync failures but they still recur during ramp-up. Startup sync failures extend the time to stable running. Diagnosing timing between carton and unit arrival needs skill. Failed sync at startup produces scrap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of incompatibility between the specified carton hierarchy and required downstream case or pallet relationships
rows.imp_rat: Incompatibility of carton hierarchy with downstream case/pallet relationships is a design/aggregation concern peripheral to insertion, with moderate consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Aggregation standards cover common case/pallet structures well, but bespoke downstream relationships still produce recurring conflicts. Aligning carton hierarchy with case/pallet relationships is a specification-time task with little runtime delay. End-to-end aggregation design requires supply-chain and serialization knowledge that remains specialist work. Downstream mismatches trigger repackaging or rejected shipments, a significant occasional cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of personnel being deployed to the wrong product run or packaging configuration
rows.imp_rat: Deploying personnel to the wrong run is an administrative staffing error peripheral to the machine's insertion job, with moderate and recoverable consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Shift briefings and qualification matrices make wrong-run deployment fairly controllable though manual. Assigning personnel to the right run is a scheduling task done ahead of shift. Correct deployment depends on the overseer's knowledge of qualifications and current schedule. Misdeployment wastes labor and can delay runs but rarely destroys product.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of discovering incompatibilities among labeled-unit geometry, carton geometry, and cartoning-machine limits after configuration recommendation
rows.imp_rat: Discovering geometry/limit incompatibilities after recommendation is a design-rework timing issue peripheral to running production, with moderate consequences.
rows.sat_rat: There is no reliable predictive check in the status quo, so incompatibilities are commonly found late rather than prevented. Discovering geometry/machine-limit incompatibilities after recommendation forces time-consuming rework loops. Foreseeing these clashes requires deep combined knowledge of unit geometry, carton design, and machine envelope that few hold. Late discovery wastes engineering effort and can require tooling changes, driving real cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that cartoning activity is attributed to the wrong batch or lot
rows.imp_rat: Wrong-batch attribution is peripheral to the physical cartoning job (low relevance) but has serious compliance/recall consequences when it occurs (moderate severity). Held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Batch-record headers and scanning reduce misattribution, though manual steps still allow errors. Wrong-batch attribution is a documentation error corrected slowly during review. Correct batch attribution depends on operator entry discipline. Misattribution causes costly record investigations.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of blocked discharge preventing completed cartons from leaving the machine
rows.imp_rat: Blocked discharge delays are throughput/availability issues peripheral to correct, safe cartoning, with modest and recoverable consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Accumulation conveyors and full-line sensors detect blockage and stop feed reliably, limiting how long discharge stays blocked. Blocked discharge stops the machine and is felt immediately as downtime until cleared. Clearing a discharge blockage is a routine operator task requiring modest skill. Downtime consumes labor and capacity, though little material is lost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation from target cartoning throughput during operation
rows.imp_rat: Throughput deviation is an efficiency/productivity concern peripheral to correct, safe cartoning; consequences are largely economic and recoverable.
rows.sat_rat: Modern cartoners with servo control and speed monitoring hold rate reasonably well, though minor stops still pull throughput off target. Throughput deviation directly manifests as lost run time, and operators monitor rate meters to react quickly. Holding target throughput requires experienced operators who anticipate feed and jam behavior. Off-target throughput wastes machine capacity and labor hours but rarely scraps material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that prior-run cartons, inserts, or sterile units remain after line clearance
rows.imp_rat: Residual prior-run units after clearance is a mix-up/contamination risk (moderate severity) but is a clearance-procedure concern peripheral to core erection/insertion (low relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Documented line-clearance checks are standard but rely on visual inspection that occasionally misses items. Line clearance to remove prior-run items adds changeover time. Thorough clearance depends on operator diligence rather than special skill. Residual prior-run items risk costly mix-up rejections.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that machine configuration does not match the approved product and carton format at startup
rows.imp_rat: Config mismatch at startup can produce wrong-format packaging (moderate relevance) with meaningful rework/compliance consequences (moderate severity), but ranked below batch 1.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe management and setup verification make configuration matching fairly reliable, with residual manual-check failures. Startup configuration checks add setup time but are routine. Verifying config against approved format requires trained setup judgement. Wrong configuration at startup wastes startup material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that prior-run cartons, leaflets, labels, or sterile units remain in the cartoning machine after line clearance

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an emergency stop fails to halt hazardous cartoning-machine motion during confirmation checks

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of exposure to electrical, pneumatic, thermal, or vacuum hazards from incorrect or leaking connections
rows.imp_rat: Exposure to electrical/pneumatic/thermal hazards is high-severity for personnel but peripheral to the packaging job itself (low relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Standardized connectors and inspection routines make exposure uncommon but leaks still occur. Connection checks add modest setup time. Identifying leaking or wrong connections needs trained technicians. Leaks waste energy/media and risk injury cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of carton blanks failing to open fully during erection

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that carton blanks, leaflets, labels, or sterile units for the wrong format are consumed during setup

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required label content becomes obscured on erected cartons

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of cartons that open, deform, or lose structural integrity under expected conditions

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that protocol scenarios fail to detect damage to labeled sterile units or their sterile barriers during insertion and carton handling

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that diagnostic handling compromises sterile unit identity, segregation, or package integrity

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that insertion compromises labeled sterile-unit package integrity during verification testing

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that replenishment introduces contamination onto packaging materials or into the cartoning area

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of prior-run fragments or residue remaining in cartoning-machine material paths after clearance

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of change parts or fasteners left loose, misaligned, or unsecured after setup

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of erected cartons with torn panels, crushed edges, or unintended creases

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a restart collision caused by cartons or sterile units left in the machine path
